data.title: American Century Value Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio Review

Equities advanced. A topsy-turvy quarter ended with stocks reaching record highs after an initial drop that carried over from the previous quarter. The rally ignited when a pause on drastic U.S. tariffs and subsequent trade deals gave investors comfort that worst-case scenarios were behind them. Investors’ increased appetite for risk sent stock markets into a fulsome rally for most of the quarter.

Technology stocks supported a sustained rally. Investors retreated to low-volatility stocks early in the quarter, but large-cap technology stocks propelled the market rally during the second half of the period. Communication services stocks also supported market gains. Health care and energy were laggards due to policy headwinds and falling oil prices, respectively.

Industrials hindered performance. Stock selection weighed on performance for the period. In particular, our choice not to own The Boeing Co. and General Electric in the aerospace and defense industry weighed on results. Elsewhere, not owning GE Vernova (GEV) also detracted as our research has indicated that the company has been riding tailwinds of increasing electrical load growth demand forecast, driven by the development of artificial intelligence data centers.

Health care hurt. Health care had a challenging quarter as regulatory and policy uncertainties, as well as tariff issues, cast a pall over the sector. Stock selection in the health care equipment and supplies industry was a drag on performance, while allocation in the pharmaceuticals industry relative to the benchmark weighed on results.

Materials aided results. Security selection in the materials sector helped performance. Light exposure to the chemicals industry compared to the benchmark was beneficial to results as benchmark companies tended to underperform during the period. Meanwhile, an overweight in the construction materials industry relative to the benchmark, helped along by a position in Martin Marietta Materials, buoyed results.

Key Contributors

UnitedHealth Group (UNH). Limited exposure to this health care company helped as it stopped providing guidance due to higher-than-expected Medicare utilization. Other setbacks included the stepping down of UnitedHealth’s CEO, a news report that said the company was under criminal investigation for alleged Medicare fraud and a plan by regulators to aggressively audit industry participants.

nVent Electric (NVT). While this electrical equipment company reported mixed earnings during the period, its shares were buoyed by growing investor enthusiasm for artificial intelligence and demand for data center development, for which nVent Electric provides a variety of products.

The Estee Lauder Cos (EL). Shares in this beauty products company rallied as global beauty product markets have stabilized. According to our research, the company has improved its market share, and management continues to align its costs base with current needs.

Key Detractors

Zimmer Biomet Holdings (ZBH). We believe this medical technology company’s shares were pressured by the prospect of higher tariffs in China and Europe. Zimmer Biomet’s growth remains slightly below industry averages, although management expects new product utilization may ramp up later this year.

Conagra Brands (CAG). Shares of this packaged-foods company fell during the period along with its industry peers, which collectively faced one of the worst quarters in years. Investors worried that inflation, particularly in meat and steel, could prove difficult for Conagra to pass through higher costs, which would constrain earnings.

GE Vernova. Not owning shares in this energy equipment manufacturing company dampened performance. GE Vernova’s shares have advanced as the need for power generation to support artificial intelligence has surged.

Notable Trades

Alphabet (GOOGL). We initiated a position in this technology company on weakness because we believe it is a high-quality company and that the market’s concerns about its ability to compete with peers in the artificial intelligence space have been overblown.

UnitedHealth Group. We initiated a position in this health care company after a period of underperformance resulted in shares trading at what we believed was an attractive entry point. Although UnitedHealth Group has faced multiple issues over the last year, we think it remains a high-quality company with favorable long-term underlying business drivers.

The Kraft Heinz Co. We sold our position in this food company as we believed market share headwinds had proven to be more challenging and broad-based than originally anticipated. We also think the departures of certain board members may signal a potential exit by Kraft Heinz’s largest shareholder.

Dollar Tree (DLTR). We exited our position in this discount retailer as we believed its shares outperformed during the period. We redeployed the funds to other positions that we thought were more attractive.

Portfolio Positioning

The portfolio seeks to invest in companies where we believe the valuation does not reflect the quality and normal earnings power of the company. Our process is based on individual security selection, but broad themes have emerged.

Value stocks may offer resilience. We think value stocks may offer resilience. Investors have continued to face macroeconomic and geopolitical risks, as well as uncertainty about economic growth, inflation and the direction the Federal Reserve may take on interest rates. Against this backdrop, we think value-oriented stocks should produce more predictable earnings and carry less valuation risk than their growth counterparts.

Opportunities in health care. Our research has led us to several health care stocks that we think offer attractive risk/reward profiles. While regulatory and policy uncertainty has weighed on the sector, we believe some concerns have been overstated, and we have identified what we think are several high-quality companies that have been trading at historically compelling valuations. We consider health care to typically be less prone to cyclical effects because the economy’s performance tends not to significantly impact demand.

Navigating the financials sector. Our position in the financials sector is now slightly underweight relative to the benchmark. We remain overweight in the banking industry but have maintained underweights in insurance and consumer finance. While banks have faced a period of weaker loan demand, we think the broad risk of their borrowers being unable to meet loan obligations remains relatively low and interest margins have stabilized. We see what we believe have been compelling opportunities in financials due to the sector’s relatively attractive valuations, moderating interest rates and a potential shift in sentiment if regulations enacted during the previous U.S. presidential administration are rolled back. We remain focused on identifying what we believe are attractively valued equities in the financials sector that can generate consistent returns from diverse sources, while maintaining strong capital and liquidity ratios and lower risk.

We continue to see value in energy. We remain overweight in what we think are high-quality diversified integrated energy companies. Despite recent cyclical weakness in oil and gas prices, we believe these companies have continued to generate significant free cash flow given their low-cost assets. Further, our research has indicated that the management teams of these businesses have structurally improved their capital allocation priorities, which has led to attractive cash returns to equity holders. We’ve also found value in energy service companies that have increased their exposure to structurally higher-growing, low-carbon energy markets.

Modestly underweight in industrials. We have lowered our underweight to industrials relative to the benchmark as we believe valuations have become more attractive. We have identified several stocks in the industrials sector that we think offer good risk/reward profiles.

Goal and Strategy

Long-term capital growth and income by investing in the stocks of companies of all sizes the managers believe to be undervalued.

data.title: MacroGenics: Struggling Wheeler- Dealer Dealt Away Its Best Bets
data.description:
This is my third MacroGenics (NASDAQ:MGNX) article after 12/2024's "MacroGenics: A Productive Biotech With Growing Potential - Still Not A Buy". I rated MacroGenics as a ("Hold") in Still Not A Buy. MacroGenics has continued to drop in price. As I write on 09/04/2025 it trades with a market cap of ~$120 million down >44% from where it was when I last wrote.

In this article I will report on whether it has become a bargain or is likely to continue its trek downward.

MacroGenics is a clinical stage biotech with a rich past of deals that have left it hollowed out.

General

MacroGenics has had successes in acquiring molecules that passed through their clinical phases on to FDA approval. Its Q2/2025 10-Q describes these as:

We and our partners are developing or commercializing product candidates for which we retain certain economic rights. These include three products approved by the FDA: MARGENZA (margetuximab-cmkb), an anti-HER2 monoclonal antibody (mAb) that we sold to a partner, ZYNYZ (retifanlimabdlwr), an anti-PD-1 mAb that we out-licensed; and TZIELD (teplizumab-mzwv), an anti-CD3 mAb that we sold to a partner. ...

The bottom half of MacroGenics' 08/14/2025 Corporate Update Presentation slide 4 lists its partnered programs as shown below:

Approved therapies

Of these five partnered programs there are three approved therapies:

  1. MARGENZA was a relative dud;
  2. ZYNYZ while not expected to reach blockbuster status is expected to generate $300-$500 million in annual revenues;
  3. TZIELD is expected to be a mega blockbusters.

With 20/20 hindsight it is clear MacroGenics should have done everything possible to hold on to hold onto ZYNYZ and TZIELD. Such was not to be, in "Still Not A Buy" I described how MacroGenics dealt out its three therapies which were ultimately approved as described below in return for cash, milestones and royalties:

  1. MARGENZA(margetuximab-cmkb) — sold to privately held TerSera Therapeutics for $40 million cash and $35.0 million milestones closed in 11/2024.
  2. ZYNYZ (retifanlimab-dlwr — 2017 deal with Incyte (INCY), which paid $150 million upfront; the deal was amended four times in 03/2018, 04/2022, 07/2022 and 07/2024, in its Q2/2025 earnings press release MacroGenics advised it remains eligible to receive up to $540.0 million in milestones, additionally the deal included royalty rights which MacroGenics just partially dealt in return for $70 million;
  3. TZIELD (teplizumab-mzwv) — 05/2018 deal for $395 million in milestones [it remains eligible for up to $379.5 million of these] plus single-digit royalty on net sale, the single digit royalty became so valuable that Macro-Genics was able to sell it in 03/08/2023 for $100 million cash plus $100 million milestones.

Gilead collaboration

On 10/17/2022 MacroGenics signed a collaboration with Gilead Sciences (GILD). Presentation slide 19 below describes MGD024 the Next Generation CD123 × CD3 DART Molecule which is subject to this agreement:

The terms of this deal are described in summary form at pps 10-12 of MacroGenics' Q2/2025 10-Q. Key terms include:

  1. development and commercialization of MGD024 to create bispecific cancer antibodies using the MacroGenics' DART platform;
  2. MacroGenics will continue its ongoing phase 1 trial for MGD024 according to a development plan;
  3. within its first two years, Gilead can nominate a bispecific cancer target for up to two research programs conducted and with separate options to license molecules created under each research program;
  4. upfront payment to MacroGenics of $60 million; plus up to $1.7 billion in target nomination, option fees, and development, regulatory and commercial milestones and tiered, low double-digit royalties on worldwide net sales of MGD024.

The agreement has been revised on several occasions. Gilead has paid MacroGenics a $15.7 million research program nomination fee.

Share impact

The price chart below shows the end result of this prolific deal making. Macrogenics is not faring well; it is on the verge of sinking down to penny stock territory.

Cash runway extends through H1/2027.

In its Q2/2025 earnings release MacroGenics advised that its cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities balance as of 06/30/2025, was $176.5 million. Its cash burn for H1/2025 was ~$94 million. Its 10-K cash flow table below for years 2024 and 2023 shows how extremely muddled cash burn and cash runway figures can get in the presence of significant milestones:

In this regard it is noteworthy that cash burn for 2024 was impacted by a $100.0 million milestone payment received from Incyte under the Incyte License Agreement; for 2023 it was impacted by $45.0 million in milestone payments received from Provention and a $15.0 million milestone payment received from Incyte.

Conclusion

As a believer in the acronym "the proof is in the pudding", I have to question the deals that Management has made generating its market cap pudding of ~$120 million and its awful share trajectory as reflected by the chart above. I thank my lucky stars that I have not been a shareholder.

For those who would trouble themselves by asking why the market is treating MacroGenics so rudely, I would submit that it is in its lack of attractive catalysts. Take a look at its strategic priorities as set out in its Q2/2025 earnings release. They are all aspirational or early stage, as shown below:

  • Determine development path for lorigerlimab based on data from the ongoing LORIKEET and LINNET studies. — early stage
  • Advance MGC026 and MGC028 programs to assess clinical proof-of-concept. — aspirational
  • Submit Investigational New Drug (IND) application for MGC030. — early stage
  • Initiate IND-enabling studies for two new product candidates. — early stage
  • Forge partnerships and collaborations to accelerate development of the Company’s proprietary product candidates and technology platforms. — aspirational
  • Improve MacroGenics’ financial position through a combination of enhanced operational efficiency, collaboration revenue, and monetization of assets. — aspirational

There is no red meat in this list. There is nothing to suggest that a market turning event is coming soon. I rate MacroGenics as a "Hold". While I am not ready to acquire shares I recognize the potential in a company that has played a role in developing two successful drugs.

data.title: J.Jill: A Risky But Rewarding Turnaround Case As Sales Trail
data.description:
J.Jill, Inc. (NYSE:JILL) reported the company’s fiscal Q2 results on the 3rd of September. The apparel retailer showed slow comparable sales, and earnings were pressured immensely through sales deleverage and higher promotional activity. The concerning performance shifts eyes to J.Jill’s assortment strategy, which aims to regain consumer interest.

I upgraded my rating on the stock to Strong Buy in the previous March article, titled “J.Jill: Stable Underlying Earnings Power At 6 Times Earnings”. The stock has since lost -7% of its value, underperforming the S&P 500’s return of 14% as J.Jill has since started to report concerning earnings.

J.Jill Q2: Slow Comparable Growth Is Threatening Earnings

J.Jill’s Q2 results were weak, even though the results beat Wall Street’s estimates. The company has previously reported slow progress in comparable sales growth with an especially weak Q1, and while the metric improved sequentially, comparable store sales growth remained weak at -1.0%. Total revenues came in down -0.8% at $154.0 million, still beating Wall Street’s very weak consensus estimate by $5.8 million.

While the consumer sentiment has remained low, the BEA’s Q2 figures still show resiliently growing spending on apparel and footwear during the April-June period. Spending in the category showed 3.8% growth in real terms - J.Jill’s underperformance isn’t explained by macroeconomic turbulence, but rather by the company’s weak ability to capture consumer interest. Other apparel companies don’t seem to have reported such comparable store weakness, as The Gap, Inc. (GAP) reported 1% growth in the metric, Urban Outfitters, Inc. (URBN) reported even stronger momentum at 5.6%, and Victoria's Secret & Co. (VSCO) showed 4% comparable growth among other apparel retailers’ much better performances.

Even though J.Jill notes that traffic improved sequentially through the summer sale period; the Q3 guidance suggests comparable sales growth to decelerate back to an even more concerning level. Comparable store sales are guided to be down low-to-mid single digits for the quarter, continuing quite a concerning streak.

Slow comparable sales are quick to pressure earnings – J.Jill’s adjusted operating income came in at $19.6 million, down by -21.3% year-on-year. The gross margin declined by 210 basis points to 68.4% due to increased promotional activity, and moderate SG&A inflation added earnings pressure on top. The quarter’s adjusted EPS of $0.81 still beat Wall Street’s consensus by $0.06, as expectations for the company are already weak after a concerning Q1 report. The Q3 adjusted EBITDA guidance range was set at $18-22 million, down significantly from $26.8 million a year ago. At the current pace, continued comparable sales declines are deteriorating J.Jill’s historically strong margins quite fast. The guidance does include a significant $5 million headwind from tariffs.

Assortment Strategy Needs to Drive a Turnaround

J.Jill is clearly trying to adapt its assortment to recapture consumer interest. The company is widening its assortment, aiming to improve relevance. The change seems to try to expand J.Jill, from just the company’s target demographic of 45-64-year old relatively affluent women, to also capture a younger customer base. Other initiatives in the assortment, marketing, and product story also aim to improve J.Jill’s concerning performance, albeit I believe that the assortment’s relevance is the most critical factor. J.Jill changed the chief merchandising officer in June to Courtney O’Connor, bringing expertise from Club Monaco, Ralph Lauren, and other apparel companies.

Ultimately, J.Jill’s ability to capture better consumer interest is uncertain. The company’s Q3 guidance suggests a weak performance, not yet suggesting any improvements. Google search volumes do show a massive improvement in J.Jill’s year-on-year search interest from July forward, but the trend doesn’t seem to have materialized in sales.

A turnaround seems likely to take time, and in the meantime, slower sales and increased promotions seem likely to pressure earnings. J.Jill’s assortment team is planning a compelling assortment for spring 2026 alongside Courtney O’Connor while the company is making more minor adjustments to near-term assortments.

There’s Still Volatile Upside in JILL Stock

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for J.Jill’s stock. I assume a significantly worse financial performance than previously.

In a base scenario, I assume J.Jill’s sales slowly stabilized, ultimately improving to a slow 1.5% perpetual growth rate. The assumptions now suggest that J.Jill will continue to struggle with generating stable consumer interest in the next few years, but that J.Jill will still ultimately stabilize its performance.

Slower sales expectations and weaker gross margins deteriorate the margin outlook. I now assume the EBIT margin to continue declining to a 9% level, down significantly from a 12.4% EBIT margin in FY2024. J.Jill’s after-tax cash flow conversion should remain good as the company is opening a minimal number of new stores.

The estimates put J.Jill’s fair value at $26.5, 62% above the stock price at the time of writing. The investment still has significant potential upside at a forward P/E of just 5.4. Upside ultimately relies on J.Jill’s ability to recapture consumer interest to stabilize earnings, which is uncertain for now—the investment’s upside is increasingly uncertain. The significantly cheap valuation does, in my opinion, make the risk-to-reward good as J.Jill is working to improve the assortment.

The fair value estimate is down from $35.7 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 10.55%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a high 15.61% interest rate, annualized from J.Jill’s Q2 interest expenses and debt. I again assume a moderate 10% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.30% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.22. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity stands at 10.44% and the WACC at 10.55%.

Takeaway

J.Jill again reported concerning sales in Q2, leading to weak Q3 guidance. The company has underperformed the industry by failing to capture consumer interest, ultimately deteriorating earnings through increased promotional activity and sales deleverage. An assortment turnaround is in the works, but results remain to be seen. The stock is risky but still provides a good risk-to-reward due to a very low valuation. I downgrade my rating on JILL stock to Buy.

data.title: Veeva Q2: Outpacing Salesforce's "Kill" Switch
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Veeva (NYSE:VEEV), I reiterated my “buy” rating on the stock with a price target of $322 per share, as Veeva saw its revenue growth accelerate for three consecutive quarters, despite management expecting a deceleration in Q1.

While the stock is down slightly since then, it is up over 27% since my “buy” rating in January 2025, beating the S&P 500 by over 2.7x.

Veeva recently reported its Q2 FY26 earnings, where revenue and earnings growth both came ahead of management’s expectations, with the revenue growth rate anchored at 17% YoY in Q2, similar to the previous quarter.

During the earnings call, Veeva also discussed their expanded opportunity in the Commercial Cloud, now that it has resolved all legal disputes and removed restrictions on putting IQCIA data into Veeva software.

However, the stock has been under pressure, and I believe it could be tied to Salesforce’s (NYSE:CRM) latest earnings call, where management said that they are “killing Veeva” in Life Sciences Cloud.

However, the way I see it, I find Salesforce’s commentary highly exaggerated, as Veeva’s strength lies in building deep industry applications. Coupled with Veeva AI, I believe that the company will have a lead in industry-specific agents, unlike Salesforce’s “toolkit-based” approach to Agentforce.

With management raising full-year FY26 guidance and forward consensus estimates projecting revenue ahead of management’s own guidance in FY31 at $6B+, I strongly believe Veeva remains a “buy” at current levels, with an updated price target of $348 per share.

Revenue growth anchored at 17%, Management raised FY26 guidance.

Veeva reported their Q2 FY26 earnings, where total revenue grew by 17% YoY to $789M, beating management’s guidance by 300 basis points.

While both its Commercial and Development Cloud continued to show strength during the quarter, the magnitude of the revenue beat was smaller compared to the previous quarter, leading to muted investor reaction to its latest earnings call.

Note, however, that on a quarter-over-quarter basis, the revenue growth rate remained anchored at 17%, instead of decelerating, like management had originally projected.

When it comes to profitability, Veeva generated $353M in non-GAAP operating income, which grew 26% YoY, with a margin expansion of 330 basis points. However, on a sequential basis, margins dipped by 140 basis points, while beating management’s expectation for the quarter by 70 basis points only.

Looking forward, Veeva has raised their forward revenue and earnings guidance, where they now expect full-year FY26 revenues to grow 14% (vs. the previous expectation of 13%) to roughly $3.137B, with a non-GAAP operating income of $1.388B (vs. the previous guidance of $1.36B), representing a margin of 44%.

What is also bullish in my opinion is that despite the stock falling after earnings, forward estimates for its revenue and earnings growth continued to be revised higher, as can be seen below.

So, when it comes to revenue, Veeva’s forward revenue for FY26 has been revised higher by 1.27% over the last month, with revenue estimates exceeding that of management’s target for FY31 at $6.35B.

Meanwhile, consensus estimates for forward earnings per share have also been revised higher, similar to revenue growth estimates, by 1.76% over the last month, as well as over the coming five years, as can be seen below.

Addressing Salesforce’s “kill” switch and Veeva’s expanded opportunity in Commercial Cloud

In Salesforce’s recent earnings, the management said that they were “killing Veeva” in Life Sciences Cloud.

However, I believe that it is highly exaggerated.

Let me explain.

Up until last year, Veeva faced a barrier in Commercial Cloud because of their OEM agreement with Salesforce at the time. This meant that they couldn’t develop specific applications that they wanted to. Since then, they have moved to Vault CRM, which is built on Veeva's Vault platform with over 100 live customers.

In fact, the capabilities in Vault CRM have now advanced well beyond what was in Veeva CRM, according to management, with growing interest from new and existing customers in connecting sales, marketing, and service with the full CRM suite. For FY26, Veeva has already locked in seven of the top 20 biopharmas that have committed to Vault CRM, while expecting an additional 20 commitments for the remainder of this year.

What is important to note is that, when it comes to Veeva, its strength lies in developing deep industry-specific applications, unlike Salesforce’s Agentforce.

What is also equally important is that Veeva is also making strong progress on adding agentic AI capabilities to its Vault platform and industry-specific AI agents in all Veeva applications as part of its Veeva AI initiative. Their first agents are on track to be released in December, with plans to deliver agents for clinical, operations, regulatory safety, quality, medical, and commercial in 2026.

As Veeva management put it, the monetization from Veeva AI will be twofold. The first layer of monetization will be the platform, so customers can create their own custom agents. The second layer of monetization will be from industry-specific agents that customers will get access to when they purchase Veeva AI.

Finally, when comparing Veeva’s billings and Salesforce’s cRPO (Current Remaining Performance Obligations), we can see that Salesforce’s forward business has fallen faster compared to Veeva, with no line of sight into further acceleration. Note, while billings and cRPO are different metrics with slightly different accounting processes, both are indicators of forward business growth for any given company.

Meanwhile, the resolution of its conflict with IQVIA means that Veeva can now fully unlock the opportunity of their Commercial Cloud, allowing deeper customer adoption through different products that include Veeva’s event management, Patient CRM, Campaign Management, data products, and more.

So far, the customer reaction to the partnership with IQVIA has been “overwhelmingly positive,” especially as they won’t need any “band-aid” solutions like they previously needed when they needed to stitch things together between Veeva and IQVIA.

With Veeva's industry vision coming together with Veeva AI, I strongly believe that the resolution with IQVIA is very timely, as it clears the growth runway for Commercial Cloud to take off, especially with the management’s 2030 revenue goal of $6B.

Pay attention to comps and macroeconomic pressures.

In the meantime, while macroeconomic pressures continue to exist, the management outlined that they haven’t seen a material impact on buying behavior or their sales pipeline. Plus, it is also important to note that their forward guidance assumes that there are no significant changes in the macroeconomic environment in the near term.

With that said, its earnings comps will get tougher from here onwards, before they improve in Q2 FY27, as can be seen below.

What this means is should management miss their revenue and earnings projections for FY26, or worse, be forced to revise them lower from potential macroeconomic turbulence, it could add significant volatility to the stock price.

Revisiting my valuation

Looking forward, I will take management's updated revenue guidance for FY26 and consensus revenue estimates for the years after that, which assume revenue to grow in the low to mid-teens range into FY31, exceeding $6B in total.

From a profitability standpoint, assuming that Veeva is able to unlock roughly 50 basis points of non-GAAP operating margin every year over the next 5 years as it drives deeper adoption of its Commercial and Development Cloud, it should generate $2.9B in non-GAAP operating income with a 46% margin.

This will translate to a present value of $1.8B when discounted at 8%. Assuming a forward price-to-earnings multiple of 30, which is roughly 1.6 times the average 10-year multiple of the S&P 500, we arrive at a price target of $348 per share, which represents an upside of 25% from its current levels.

My final verdict and conclusions

I believe that the post-earnings dip could be used as a buying opportunity for both new and existing investors, as I believe that fears related to Salesforce’s “killing Veeva” are highly exaggerated. Along with that, the long-term growth trajectory of Veeva just got a boost with the resolution of its conflict with IQVIA.

We can already see that with the uptrend in forward revenue and earnings revision for the company in the coming years.

As a result, I will reiterate my “buy” rating on Veeva with an updated price target of $348 per share.

data.title: Tariffs Are Taxes: Ergo, Tax Cuts Are Coming
data.description:
Thesis Summary

When President Donald Trump first unveiled his plans for tariffs, markets crashed.

But now that it seems tariffs could be reversed, the reaction from markets has been kind of muted.

If tariffs are taxes, something I’ve argued before, then the swift reversal of all tariffs could be seen as a huge tax cut on American corporations and consumers and even fiscal stimulus.

We discuss below the implications for markets and how investors should position.

Tariffs Are Taxes

While tariffs are often touted as a way to fill the Treasury coffers by taxing foreign companies, the truth is that they are ultimately a tax on consumption, which is largely paid by US consumers and corporations.

I talked about this extensively before.

But in the longer term, tariffs are deflationary, since they act as a tax. The bottom line is that tariffs take money out of the consumer's and/or the company’s pocket, and put it into the government coffers.

Source: Ignore Trump At Your Own Risk

Either through smaller profit margins or higher prices, the US private sector is paying the price, which may create some inflation in the short term but actually is deflationary in the longer term.

Will Tariffs Get Reversed?

This summer brought a pivotal shift. A U.S. federal court ruled that certain tariff collections had overstepped legal authority, opening the door to potential reversals and even refunds.

President Donald Trump has now asked the U.S. Supreme Court to overturn a federal appeals court ruling that struck down much of his sweeping tariff program as unconstitutional.

The lower court, in a 7–4 decision, found that Trump’s use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to justify broad import taxes exceeded presidential authority, calling tariff-setting “a core Congressional power.”

If upheld, the ruling could force the U.S. to refund billions collected from businesses and foreign partners.

No Tariffs=Fiscal Stimulus

The way I see it, there seems to be a good chance that tariffs could be deemed illegal, which would be akin to a significant tax cut and even a direct form of stimulus.

Though it is unclear how this would play out, corporations could get billions in payback from the taxed revenues.

Meanwhile, consumers could eventually see prices come back down as tariffs are removed. On the other hand, some sectors could even keep the new post-tariff prices, increasing their margins.

What Could This Mean For Markets?

After the liberation day tariff announcement, markets saw a brutal sell-off.

What's quite interesting is that markets quickly reversed. First, they did it in the hopes that tariffs would be delayed and trade deals would be reached.

While many trade deals have been struck, there are plenty of tariffs in effect right now.

All of that revenue is essentially money that has been taken from the private sector. The effects have not been felt yet, but as I argued before, this could eventually trickle down.

Reversing the tariffs now would no doubt be bullish for the economy, providing a form of stealth QE that the market may not have priced in.

Risks

With that said, the market is already at an all-time high. Perhaps the market already priced all of this in?

Tariffs may or may not be overruled, but if they are, there’s also the possibility that the current administration could seek other ways of reducing the deficit.

This could bring in further political uncertainty and volatility into markets.

Final Thoughts

With midterms next year, it’s going to be an interesting few months in markets. The administration has an incentive to keep investors happy. Would a reversal in tariffs help, though?

Economically speaking, I would say so, but it would also be a hit to the credibility of the administration, which could have worse implications further down the road.

data.title: Nebius Group: 3 Reasons Why It's A Fantastic Long-Term Pick
data.description:
The Nebius Group Investment Thesis

I see the Nebius Group N.V. (NASDAQ:NBIS) as a fantastic long-term opportunity to benefit from the AI market. The management team knows what it's doing, has years of experience and has some very promising projects that could generate growth, even if the boom in the field of AI infrastructure subsides. I also believe that the shares are attractively valued due to the impending rapid growth. So I want to initiate coverage with a strong buy rating.

Reason 1: The management team

Yandex, a company often referred to as the Google (GOOG) of Russia, is something like the unofficial predecessor of the Nebius Group. Arkady Volozh, the CEO and founder of Nebius, was also one of the founders of Yandex and Roman Chernin, who is now the Chief Business Officer at Nebius, was the leader of the search department at Yandex. After the attack on Ukraine in 2022, Arkady Volozh, who had been living in Israel since 2014, began negotiating with the Russian state on how to divest his Yandex shares. In the end, they agreed on a price of $5 billion.

But Arkady Volozh and Roman Chernin aren't the only high-profile Yandex employees who have left for other countries. So Nebius is one of the employers that has hired some of the most highly educated Russian experts. And as education is highly valued in Russia, these employees are among the most highly trained in the world.

Andrey Korolenko and Danila Shtain, who were responsible for Yandex's cloud and AI infrastructure business, are now also working at Nebius. Therefore, expertise in this area is definitely present. This alone sets Nebius apart from other competitors who have much less experience in the AI and cloud industries and cannot access such employees.

To gain a foothold in the important American market, Marc Borodistky was hired as Chief Revenue Officer. He was previously employed at Oracle (ORCL) and is therefore very familiar with the US cloud market. So he certainly has a lot of knowledge and contacts that could be very important in this context.

Nebius is therefore a kind of bet on the knowledge and experience that the team has gained through Yandex in the areas of cloud, search and AI, as well as on the knowledge of the world's ninth most populous country, Russia. Since most of these employees are now based in Amsterdam and Israel I can see Nebius dominating Europe and the Middle East in the long term and gaining market share in America with its full-stack AI platform.

Reason 2: Nebius's additional bets.

Often, people talk about Alphabet's 'Other Bets' or Meta's (META) many side projects, as many of them look very promising and could potentially be future growth drivers. And we have also seen how such a side bet can grow into something very big, as with Amazon (AMZN) and its cloud business. At Nebius, there are currently side bets in the form of Clickhouse, Avride, TripleTen and Toloka, in which they hold various equity stakes and which often originate from the Yandex empire.

Clickhouse is an open-source database management system designed for machine learning and real-time analysis. With an annualized revenue run rate of $96 million, up from $50 million in 2024, Clickhouse is clearly still in a strong growth phase. Furthermore, Nebius follows Oracle's playbook to some extent because Oracle has a database management system as well.

TripleTen, on the other hand, is an education platform with a focus on coding, AI, and tech. They compete with other bootcamp providers as well as MOOC platforms. But this is a niche area that will probably experience significant growth. So in combination with the Nebius ecosystem, something could be created in the future that provides access to resources that other competitors do not have. After all Nebius is probably one of the best in the field of AI.

Avride is an autonomous mobility company that is now headquartered in Austin, Texas, and competes with Waymo. Through its partnership with Uber Technologies (UBER), in which Avride's robots deliver food, the way has also been paved for Avride's cars to potentially be found on Uber soon. This means that Nebius is also active in the markets for self-driving cars and robots, both of which offer great future opportunities.

However, Nebius's most promising investment right now is Toloka, an AI data company that specializes in content collection, specific datasets, as well as training and data for AI models. And the significant role these companies play in the current AI landscape is evident in Meta's investment in Scale AI, which is relatively similar to Toloka. Additionally, the investment by Jeff Bezos's firm, Bezos Expeditions, as well as by Shopify's CTO, shows that there is apparently investor interest in Toloka. However, it should be noted that Nebius no longer has controlling voting power over Toloka, but remains a shareholder. But I strongly suspect that they will continue to benefit from Toloka's technologies and that their shares will increase in value.

Reason 3: The future and AI

Nebius's plan is to reach at least 1 GW of contracted power by the end of 2026 by expanding its operations in Finland and New Jersey and establishing a presence in two additional unnamed U.S. locations. Currently, AI infrastructure is a bottleneck, and the prices for the in-demand GPUs are relatively high, and the demand exceeds the supply. So it's an asset-heavy industry that requires a lot of financing.

Unfortunately, Nebius does not have acces to Yandex money, and some competitors have more funding, which puts Nebius at a slight disadvantage. After all, having access to a large number of the most powerful GPUs is currently a competitive advantage for companies. However, since Nebius specializes in start-ups and medium-sized companies, they still have a continuously growing customer base. And they are establishing their niche, in which they hopefully become a leader.

As switching costs in this industry are relatively high and customers are relatively locked in, I think Nebius's revenue should be sticky. Additionally, Nebius plan to offer a full-stack AI platform means that recurring revenue will be generated through the software part and this is an advantage over a pure hardware infrastructure provider.

As it currently seems likely that Inference will overtake Training, it will be interesting to see if there will be Inference as a Service providers in the future that will generate huge revenues, and if Nebius will be able to benefit from this. As things stand now, I believe that they will.

Due to its rapid growth rates, Nebius is currently valued much higher than the competition, which grows much more slowly. Established companies like Alphabet, Oracle, and AMD (AMD) trade at 7x to 9x their revenue, while rapidly growing companies like NVIDIA (NVDA) are valued at 20x their revenue. Therefore, I strongly believe that Nebius multiple will decrease in the future and will probably settle between 10x and 15x in the next three years, as long as the growth does not completely stop.

If we take the revenue estimates from Seeking Alpha as a basis, we can see that the estimates for the 2027 financial year suggest that revenues could reach $3.11 billion. This would correspond to a market value of ~$31 billion at a PS multiple of 10x and ~$46 billion at a PS multiple of 15x. Both results represent a significant upside from the current market cap of $15 billion. So I see the shares as significantly undervalued, as the market has not yet adequately considered their potential.

Risks

As Nebius operates in a highly capital-intensive industry, the company naturally requires substantial capital. This puts a strain on the cash balance, as the company has not yet generated positive cash flows. Therefore, I assume that the current cash balance of $1.6 billion is insufficient and that new debts must be incurred or other ways must be found to obtain fresh capital.

Therefore, I assume that the debt position will increase in the future, which will make the balance sheet more uncertain. Additionally, the interest burden will probably increase and depending on the debt ratio, it could also become more expensive to obtain new capital.

Additionally, there is the possibility of share dilution, as there are currently 7.5 million shares that can be exercised by employees at an average price of $87, as well as 6.7 million shares that will be granted once the vesting conditions are met. Plus there are 6.8 million shares in connection with Avride and 123 million shares in the treasury that have not yet been retired. So shareholder dilution is relatively safe, but it's not yet clear what form it will take.

Conclusion

Nebius's core business alone has fantastic prospects. However, when you combine this with the other bets that lie in Nebius's black box, I think the upside is even higher than current analyst estimates suggest. This is because this is also like a bet placed on the AI knowledge that has been dormant in Russia, specifically at Yandex. Therefore, I see Nebius as a very attractive prospect with a lot of upside.

data.title: Campbell's: Excellent Value In Frothy Market
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SPY) recently hit another all-time high, and this furthers the refrain that stocks are too expensive. However, it’s worth keeping in mind that the market-weighted index is dominated by a handful of tech stocks, with $4-trillion market cap NVIDIA (NVDA) alone accounting for 8% of the entire SPY. On the other hand, a great number of stocks in economically essential categories such as consumer staples trade at bargain basement valuations.

Such I find the case with Campbell’s Company (NASDAQ:CPB), which I last covered in February, highlighting its strong market share and improving profitability through cost savings initiatives. The stock continues to trade weakly, with it falling by 37% over the past 12 months and trading at close to a 10-year low, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit CPB including recent business results, and discuss why now may be an excellent time to layer into this durable consumer staple stock before market sentiment turns around, so let’s get started!

Why CPB?

Campbell’s Company is a global food company that offers more than just its namesake soups. It’s comprised of two divisions, including Meals & Beverages, which houses Campbell’s soups, Swanson broths, and Prego’s and Rao’s pasta sauces. CPB’s Snacks division is comprised of well-recognizable brands like Pepperidge Farm, Goldfish, Kettle Brand, and Snyder’s of Hanover, to name a few.

CPB’s growth in recent years has been helped by strategic acquisitions including Sovos, which brought with it the fast-growing Rao’s pasta sauce brand. As shown below, CPB’s revenue has fully recovered from its post-pandemic slump to reach $10.2 billion over the trailing 12 months.

CPB’s recently released fiscal Q4 2025 results (ended August 3rd) demonstrated resilience in the face of a cautious consumer environment. Organic sales were down by 3% YoY due to shipment timing reversals and portfolio divestitures (Pop Secret and Noosa). Meals & Beverages outperformed snacks, with 1% YoY consumption growth during Q4 and 2% growth for the full year. This was aided by at-home cooking trends as consumers sought to save money amidst inflation.

Broth and condensed soups drove momentum, offsetting weakness in the Condensed and RTS (ready to serve) categories, as shown below. Plus, Rao’s returned to high single-digit growth late in the year. Together with Prego, CPB has dominance over the Italian Sauces category with the #1 (Rao’s) and #2 (Prego) brands.

Snacks consumption fell 2% YoY in Q4 but saw sequential improvement. Goldfish showed early signs of stabilization and Milano cookies delivered standout growth on the strength of its White Chocolate innovation. As a result, Milano consumption grew by 27% YoY and was the number 1 driver for category dollar and volume growth over the last 2 consecutive quarters.

Admittedly, CPB’s near-term outlook isn’t so rosy, as management is guiding for flat organic sales in fiscal 2026. At the same time, Adjusted EPS is expected to be down by 15% in 2026 at the midpoint of range due to a $0.04 hit from divestitures, and a more material hit from tariffs (higher steel and aluminum costs). Management expects to mitigate around 60% of tariff impact through supplier collaboration, alternatives sourcing, productivity.

Despite near term uncertainty around costs, CPB is positioning itself for growth beyond next year with product innovation and premiumization. This is reflected by strong traction in its Milano variations and by the success of Rao’s. Moreover, CPB plans to rollout ‘better-for-you’ snacks like Kettle avocado oil chips and is committed to removing FD&C colors across the portfolio by the end of next year. This helps CPB to better align with changing consumer preferences toward healthier options with fewer processed ingredients.

Importantly, management remains committed to shareholder returns, as it raised the dividend by 5% this year after keeping it flat since 2021. CPB currently yields 4.8% and the dividend is very well-covered by a 51% payout ratio. As shown below, CPB’s yield currently sits at one of its highest points in history.

(Note: The following chart shows TTM yield. Forward yield is 4.8%)

Lastly, I continue to see value in CPB at the current price of $32.66 with a forward PE of 13.0, sitting well below its historical PE of 16.4, as shown below.

The forward PE includes EPS headwinds in FY26 (ending July 2026) and over the long run, I would expect for CPB to achieve mid to high single digit EPS growth annually. This could be supported by brand innovation and a return to normalization as tariff impacts are absorbed by the market. With a 4.8% dividend yield, mid-single digit EPS growth annually as a base case, and a potential return to normal valuation, CPB could deliver potentially strong total returns from here.

Risks to CPB include its currently elevated net debt to EBITDA ratio of 3.6x, sitting above the 3x range generally considered safe. Nonetheless, CPB has ample liquidity with $132 million in cash and $1.5 billion in undrawn capacity on its revolving credit line. Other risks include continued shifts in consumer tastes, which could put pressure on continued brand innovation toward healthier options. Moreover, increased competition from cheaper private label brands in an inflationary environment could put pressure on product pricing.

Investor Takeaway

Campbell’s appears to be flying under the radar, with its current price weakness presenting a compelling opportunity for value investors. Despite short-term headwinds from tariffs and flat near-term sales guidance, CPB is leveraging strong brands to drive long-term growth through innovation and premiumization.

The stock trades near a decade low with a forward PE of just 13, offering an attractive entry point compared to its historical average. Lastly, CPB has a well-covered 4.8% dividend yield, improving product mix, and potential for multiple expansion as market sentiment shifts. As such, CPB stock offers a combination of income, stability, and upside potential in a frothy market.

data.title: BBVA: Still A Buy After An Impressive Share Price Performance
data.description:
Introduction

I rarely label stocks a ‘strong buy’ but one of the few times I did was on this 2022 article on Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria (NYSE:BBVA), the large Spanish bank with a focus on Spain and LatAm. We are now three years later and the total return of the position is coming close to 400% (in USD terms).

I generally like banks and although most investors were very unhappy about owning Spanish banks at a certain time, I do think a lesson was learned (the hard way) in the global financial crisis and the sovereign debt crisis. But I also wanted to make sure I can take profits of the table if warranted, so I wanted to have a closer look at BBVA’s H1 results to see if I need to change my approach.

A strong Q2 result allows for a guidance increase

In the second quarter of the current financial year, BBVA reported a total net interest income of just over €6.2B which is somewhat lower than in the first quarter and compared to the second quarter last year, but looking at the H1 net interest income of €12.6B, we see that although this represents a 3% decrease on a reported basis, it actually suggests a 9.7% increase on a constant exchange rate basis.

Fortunately the bank’s net fees and commissions increased by 4.4% on a reported basis, and this helped to show a gross income increase of 3.4% on a reported basis (and 19.6% on a constant currency basis).

The total operating income was approximately €11.25B, a 6.2% increase, while the total amount of loan loss provisions remained relatively stable at around €2.8B. This ultimately helped to report a pre-tax income of €8.4B and a net profit of €5.8B of which €351M was attributable to non-controlling interests. This means the net profit attributable to the shareholders of BBVA was approximately €5.45B for an EPS of €0.91. And that’s an 8% increase compared to the €0.84 EPS realized in the first semester of last year.

This also adds more credibility to the bank’s guidance for the 2025-2028 timeframe. As previously disclosed, BBVA aims to generate a Return on Tangible Equity of 22% which should allow the tangible book value and dividend per share to increase by a ‘mid teens percentage’ for the next few years. Additionally, the bank assumes to total net profit to be around €48B.

The bank also published what it exactly plans to do with these profits. As shown below, it expects to generate €39B in CET1 capital during the 2025-2028 time frame while it expects to unlock an additional €5B through securitizations. And as it started the year with an excess amount of €4.5B above the required CET1 capital ratio of 12%, BBVA has about €49B in anticipated ‘excess’ capital.

As you can see below, about €13B will be used to invest in growth. That €13B in additional growth could add about €100-110B in growth to the total amount of risk-weighted assets (which stood at about €387B at the end of June). And this will of course help to fuel the earnings.

The bank is adhering to its 50% payout ratio for the ‘normal’ dividends, and this will absorb €24B. Divided over the current share count of 5.76B, this implies the bank will pay almost €4.2 per share in dividends. But as I also expect BBVA to complete additional share repurchase programs, the final accumulated dividend per share will likely be higher than that, and could be closer to €5/share, depending on the timing and pace of those buybacks.

BBVA is still trying to acquire Banco de Sabadell and this effort is weighing on its current financial results. I do think Sabadell would be a good target for BBVA. Unfortunately it may have to increase its bid to successfully acquire the smaller bank, as the Sabadell share price is now trading above the fair value of the BBVA offer.

Risks to the thesis

These are all nice plans but a strong guidance still doesn't offer guarantees. BBVA remains subject to the general wellbeing of the financial markets, and a recession in some of the countries it is active in would have a negative impact on its expectations. As Mexico is an important area where BBVA is active, the Mexico-US trade relationship will be an important element to keep an eye on.

Additionally, as shown in the H1 2025 results, the exchange rate fluctuations could also have a major impact on the bank's financial results, as it reports its results in Euro. Investors should be mindful of these risks and keep an eye on the quarterly updates to ensure the results are still in line with the expectations.

Investment thesis

When I started reviewing the bank’s H1 financials, I thought I’d end up with a ‘hold’ rating. But that doesn’t appear to be the case as BBVA plans to generate €48B in net income or €8.4 per share in net income in the 2025-2028 period. This represents an average EPS of north of €2 per share (with an anticipated EPS of €1.80 this year and closer to €1.90-1.95 per share next year as the balance sheet expands).

This means the stock is actually still not expensive, but because I already have a decent position (I entered, exited and re-entered a few times in the past few years, by writing put options) I may also sell some shares. And that makes the conclusion of this article a bit weird as based on the numbers, BBVA still is a ‘buy’. As you may have noticed, I have written a few articles here on Seeking Alpha on some US regional banks, and I want to deploy some of the cash into those names. BBVA is well-managed and well-run, but I need to take some money off the table and deploy it elsewhere, despite rating the stock a ‘buy’.

data.title: IBM: A Great Business At Just A Fair Price (Downgrade)
data.description:
One really solid business over the last several years now has been technology giant IBM (NYSE:IBM). Even though those who don't follow technology closely might view this enterprise as a relic of the past, the fact of the matter is that this is a massive and continuously growing enterprise. As of this writing, it boasts a market capitalization of $228.08 billion. That makes it not only one of the largest technology companies but also one of the largest companies in the world, period.

Even though financial performance has been a bit lumpy over the years, the general trend for cash flows has been higher. In the past, I had been rather bullish about the company. In fact, in my last article about it, written quite long ago in July 2019, I looked at the $1 trillion opportunity that IT and telecommunications conglomerate AT&T (T) had decided to tackle together that took the form of a multi-year strategic alliance to help modernize AT&T’s internal software applications while simultaneously allowing the firms to focus together in the hybrid cloud space. At the end of the day, I ended up rating IBM a "Buy." And since then, the stock is up 137%, which is comfortably above the 117.9% rise the S&P 500 has seen over the same period.

Looking at the picture today, I see that the overall position of IBM is positive. The company continues to grow, though that growth has certainly been lumpy. Shares are not expensive on an absolute basis. But relative to other companies that have similarities to it, they are a bit pricey. Given this combination of factors, I think it would be a wise decision to downgrade IBM to a "Hold" at this time.

Taking a fresh look at IBM

One thing I can say about IBM that I definitely think about in a favorable light is that management continues to achieve revenue growth over time. From 2022 through 2024, revenue for the company expanded from $60.53 billion to $62.75 billion. And for the first half of this year, the $31.52 billion generated by the company beat out the $30.23 billion reported one year earlier. With a business as large and complex as this, it wouldn't be good to just talk about, using broad strokes, what caused this revenue increase. Rather, we would be wise to look at each of the firm's operating segments in turn. Doing so, we actually find some growth areas for the company, as well as an area that has been struggling in recent years.

The heart and soul of the company is its Software business, which generates revenue from its hybrid platform and solutions products, which include its Red Hat business, automation services, data and AI activities, and security offerings. It also includes its transaction processing activities. Over these last three years, revenue for this segment expanded from $23.63 billion to $27.09 billion. This seems to be driven by growth across its hybrid platform and solutions operations, as well as its transaction processing activities. In the former case, the business is enjoying continued demand for its hybrid cloud solutions as its customers prioritize application modernization using OpenShift containers. Ansible automation is also being used to optimize their IT spending and reduce operational complexity.

If you are new to the company, OpenShift is a platform developed by Red Hat that allows customers to build, deploy, and manage containerized applications at scale. Ansible, meanwhile, which is also under the Red Hat banner, focuses more on IT automation, configuration management, and orchestration. This, in particular, helps customers with automating repetitive IT tasks, but it also involves configuring hybrid cloud infrastructure, enforcing continuous security and compliance, and more.

These two technologies actually are highly complementary, since OpenShift essentially houses and helps to scale the software application, while Ansible provides the automation layer. This kind of technology is incredibly useful across industries. Financial institutions like banks, for instance, can utilize micro services architectures for mobile banking and payment activities, while also improving things like real-time fraud detection. Hospitals, as well as insurers, benefit differently. Many of them deal with old legacy technologies, which aren't very user-friendly or efficient. By using these types of services, those types of customers can achieve a faster rollout when it comes to telemedicine applications, patient portals, and more.

The segment has also been benefiting from growth in the data and AI category, with its AI assistant for customer care, as well as other features, experiencing attractive demand. SaaS subscription offerings like AIOps and Management are also growing. Meanwhile, transaction processing revenue is also on the rise, with growth attributable to strong renewal rates, additional revenue opportunities created by the firm's generative AI products, and what management refers to as the "growing capacity demands" of clients.

By comparison, revenue from the Consulting segment has been a bit lumpier. After rising from $20.06 billion in 2022 to $20.88 billion in 2023, there was a drop to $20.69 billion in 2024. This decline came as technology consulting revenue dropped 3.2% year over year, while applications operations revenue fell 3%. The good news is that, for the 2025 fiscal year, we have started to see a recovery. Revenue of $10.38 billion beat out the $10.37 billion reported the previous year. There have been some changes that the company has made to the firm's operations this year. So we don't exactly have great comparability data. But we do know that what management now calls the intelligent operations side of the business, benefited from cloud platform engineering services and application management services that more than offset, just barely, weaker strategy and technology revenue.

The other big segment that IBM has is its Infrastructure segment, which generates revenue from hybrid infrastructure activities and certain infrastructure Support Solutions. This has also been a source of growth for the company this year, but that is honestly something that's new. From 2022 through 2024, its revenue fell from $15.29 billion to $14.02 billion. This year, the recovery to $7.03 billion compared to $6.72 billion can be attributed to the hybrid infrastructure activities that the company engages in. In particular, IBM Z, which is a service that helps in a wide array of ways, such as helping to handle high-volume, high-speed transactions for financial institutions and stock exchanges, helping on the security and compliance side by providing pervasive encryption of data, and by offering up built-in AI inferencing technologies that help companies focus on things like fraud detection, risk scoring, and more, saw revenue expand by 36.8%.

There are other things that the company engages in as well, such as its much smaller Financing segment that helps to facilitate client acquisition of hardware, software, and services, that the company provides its customers. It also engages in alternative arrangements like leasing out technologies to customers. And on the commercial side of things, it provides short-term working capital financing to its business partners and distributors of the products and services that the technology giant sells. Revenue here has been quite lumpy. But for the most part, it has remained range-bound over the last three years at between $645 million and $741 million.

As the chart above illustrates, net profits have been all over the map for the business. Some cash flow metrics have also been lumpy. But when we look at adjusted operating cash flow and EBITDA, the trend is clear. As management has overseen this stable cash flow growth, they have also made clear their objective to return capital directly to shareholders. This has primarily taken the form of dividends, with overall dividends from 2022 through the second quarter of 2025 amounting to an impressive $21.25 billion.

If we project out results that we have seen so far for the year, we would expect adjusted operating cash flow to come in at around $12.95 billion. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business would be around $15.98 billion. In the chart below, you can see how the company is valued using these estimates, and how it's valued using historical results from the 2024 fiscal year. These multiples put the company pretty solidly in the fair value range, as I see it. But relative to other similar firms, the stock is actually quite pricey. In the same chart below, you can see how the firm stacks up against other similar enterprises. Across the board, it is the most expensive of the group.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I believe IBM is doing a fine job. Some of the profitability metrics of the company are lumpier than I would like them to be. But outside of that, I don't really have any complaints. If shares were cheaper, both on an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies, I would very likely keep it rated a "Buy." But as things stand, I believe that downgrading it to a "Hold" makes the most sense at this time.

data.title: SPX 0DTE Options Jump To Record 62% Share In August
data.description:
By Mandy Xu

Cross-Asset Volatility: Implied volatilities gained modestly across asset classes last week, albeit from extremely low levels. Gold was the biggest mover, with GLD 1M implied volatility gaining over 2.2 vol pts wk/wk to 16.2% - the only asset class where volatility is trading above its long-term average currently (see Exhibit 1). The jump in GLD implied volatility is especially notable as realized volatility has collapsed to near a 1-year low of 10.4%. The 1M implied-realized spread, at 5.8%, screens in the 98th percentile high over the past year. Much of the demand for GLD optionality has come from the call side, driving a further inversion in the skew (i.e. calls trading at a premium to puts). GLD 1M skew, for example, is now in the 5th percentile low. Equity volatility (VIX®Index) increased just over 1 pt to 15.4% ahead of this Friday’s NFP number. SPX options are implying ~1.4% move for Friday. Notably, interest rate volatility (both the MOVE Index as well as VIXTLT Index) are trading near 1-year lows as we head into the much-anticipated Fed meeting later this month, with a rate cut an almost foregone conclusion at this point (~90% implied probability).

Exhibit 1: Asset Class Implied Volatilities

Equity Volatility: Non-US indices led the increase in volatility last week, with MXEA 1M implied vol up 1.1 vol pts wk/wk vs. SPX 1M implied vol up 0.6 pt. Post NVDA’s earnings, single stock volatility has dropped to near a 1-year low, with VIXEQ Index down 1.4 pts last week to 32%. NVDA’s tepid results capped a month of underperformance for Tech, with S&P equal-weight index (SPEQX) outperforming SPX index for the first time since March. Notably, while SPEQX realized volatility has fallen relative to SPX, the implied vol spread between the two has widened in recent weeks (see Exhibit 2). Cboe recently added PM-settled weekly and month-end options on SPEQX (see more here) for those looking to express views on the equal-weight index.

Exhibit 2: SPEQX vs. SPX Index Volatility

Term Structure: SPX term structure flattened modestly last week, driven by the front-end, with the 1Y-1M vol spread narrowing from 4.6% to 4.3%, though remaining extremely steep in the 88th percentile high.

0DTE Options: August was another record month for SPX 0DTE options trading, averaging ~2.4M contracts a day (see Exhibit 3) and making up a record 62.4% of overall SPX volume. The flow was fairly balanced between retail vs. institutional investors, with retail traders making up an estimated 53% of the volume. Interestingly, retail flow has tilted more toward single leg puts/calls (~52% of trades) while institutional traders still favor multi-leg strategies (~55% of trades are complex orders). For more on SPX 0DTE options trading, see here and here.

Exhibit 3: SPX 0DTE Options Set New Record in August

Cross-Asset Volatility Monitor

Cross-Asset Volatility Snapshot (10Y Lookback)

How to read this chart:

The chart shows how various asset class implied volatilities are trading relative to their own history, using a 10-year lookback.

The volatilities are ranked by z-scores, which compares the current volatility level for an asset versus its own 10-year history. A z-score of +2, for example, would mean that the current volatility level is trading 2 standard deviations above its long-term average (i.e. rich) while a z-score of -2 would mean it’s trading 2 standard deviations below average (i.e. cheap).

Because each asset class volatility trades at different levels and often defined differently (e.g. lognormal vs. normal vol, price vs. bps vol), by standardizing using z-scores, we’re able to compare different asset class volatilities on the same scale - and see at a glance if there are any divergences or dislocations in the cross-asset volatility markets.
